17.20: for thou bringest in certain new things to our ears. We will know therefore what these things may mean.
17.21: (And all the Athenians, and the strangers sojourning there, employed themselves to nothing else but either to speak, or to hear some news.)
17.22: But Paul standing in the midst of Areopagus, said: Ye men of Athens, in all things I perceive you as it were superstitious.
17.23: For passing by and seeing your idols, I found an altar also where upon was written: To the unknown God. That therefore which you worship, not knowing it, the same do I preach to you.
17.24: The God that made the world and all things that are in it, he being Lord of Heaven & earth dwelleth not in temples made with hand,
17.25: neither is he served with men's hands, needing any thing, whereas himself giveth life unto all, and breathing, and all things:
17.26: and he made of one, all mankind, to inhabit upon the whole face of the earth, assigning set times, and the limits of their habitation,
17.27: for to seek God, if happily they may feel or find him; although he be not far from every one of us:
17.28: For in him we live and move and be, as certain also of your own poets said, For of his kind also we are.
17.29: Being therefore of God's kind, we may not suppose, the Divinity to be like unto gold or silver, or stone, the graving of art and device of man.
17.30: And the times truly of this ignorance whereas God despised, now he denounceth unto men that all every-where do penance,
17.31: for that he hath appointed a day wherein he will judge the world in equity, by a man whom he hath appointed, giving all men faith, raising him up from the dead.
17.32: And when they had heard the resurrection of the dead, certain indeed mocked, but certain said: We will hear thee again concerning this point.
17.33: So Paul went forth out of the midst of them.
17.34: But certain men joining unto him, did believe: among whom was also Dionysius Areopagita, and a woman named Damaris, and others with them.

18.1: After these things, departing from Athens, he came to Corinth.
18.2: and finding a certain Jew, named Aquila, born in Pontus, who of late was come out of Italy, & Priscilla his wife (because Claudius had commanded all Jews to depart from Rome,) he came to them.
18.3: And because he was of the same craft, he remained with them, and wrought, (and they were tent-makers by their craft.)
18.4: And he disputed in the synagogue every Sabboth, interposing the name of our Lord Jesus, and he exhorted the Jews and the Greeks.
18.5: And when Silas and Timothee were come from Macedonia, Paul was instant in preaching, testifying to the Jews that Jesus is Christ.
18.6: But they contradicting and blaspheming, he shaking his garments, said to them: Your blood upon your own head: I being clean, from henceforth will go to the Gentiles.
18.7: And departing thence, he entered into the house of a certain man, named Titus Justus, one that served God, whose house was adjoining to the synagogue.
18.8: And Crispus the prince of the Synagogue believed our Lord, with all his house: & many of the Corinthians hearing believed, and were baptized.
18.9: And our Lord said in the night by a vision to Paul: Do not fear, but speak, and hold not thy peace,
18.10: for-because I am with thee: and no man shall set upon thee to hurt thee: for I have much people in this city.
18.11: And he sat there a year & six months, teaching among them the word of God.
18.12: But Gallio being Proconsul of Achaia, the Jews with one accord rose up against Paul, and brought him to the judgement seat,
18.13: saying: That this man contrary to the Law persuadeth men to worship God.
18.14: And Paul beginning to open his mouth, Gallio said to the Jews: If it were some unjust thing, or an heinous fact, O you men Jews, I should by reason bear you.
18.15: But if they be questions of word and names, and of your law, yourselves look unto it: I will not be judge of these things.
18.16: And he drove them from the judgement seat.
18.17: And all apprehending Sosthenes the prince of the Synagogue, strook him before the judgement seat: and Gallio cared for none of those things.
18.18: But Paul when he had stayed yet many days, taking his leave of the brethren, sailed to Syria, (and with him Priscilla and Aquila,) who had shorn his head in Cenchris. For he had a vow.
18.19: And he came unto Ephesus, and them he left there. But himself entering into the Synagogue, disputed with the Jews.
18.20: And when they desired him, that he would tarry a longer time, he consented not,
18.21: but taking his leave, and saying: I will return to you again God willing, he departed from Ephesus.
18.22: And going down to Cæsarea, he went up, and saluted the Church, and came down to Antioch.
18.23: And having tarried there a certain time, he departed, walking in order through the country of Galatia and Phrygia, confirming all the Disciples.
18.24: And a certain Jew, named Apollo, born at Alexandria, an eloquent man, came to Ephesus, mighty in the Scriptures.
18.25: This man was taught the way of our Lord: and being fervent in spirit he spake, & taught diligently those things that pertain to Jesus, knowing only the Baptism of John.
18.26: This man therefore began to deal confidently in the Synagogue. Whom when Priscilla and Aquila had heard, they took him unto them, and expounded to him the way of our Lord more diligently.
18.27: And whereas he was desirous to go to Achaia, the brethren exhorting wrote to the Disciples to receive him. Who, when he was come, profited them much that had believed.
18.28: For he with vehemency convinced the Jews openly, shewing by the scriptures, that Jesus is Christ.

19.1: And it came to pass when Apollo was at Corinth, that Paul having gone through the higher parts came to Ephesus, and found certain Disciples:
19.2: and he said to them: Have you received the Holy Ghost, believing? But they said to him: Nay, neither have we heard whether there be a Holy Ghost.
19.3: But he said: In what then were you baptized? Who said, In John's Baptism.
19.4: And Paul said: John baptized the people with the Baptism of penance, saying, That they should believe in him that was to come after him, that is to say, in Jesus.
19.5: Hearing these things, they were baptized in the name of our Lord Jesus.
19.6: And when Paul had imposed hands on them, the Holy Ghost came upon them, & they spake with tongues, and prophesied.
19.7: And all the men were about twelve.
19.8: And entering into the Synagogue, he spake confidently for three months, disputing & exhorting of the Kingdom of God.
19.9: But when certain were indurate, and believed not, ill-speaking the way of our Lord before the multitude, departing from them, he separated the Disciples, daily disputing in the school of one Tyrannus.
19.10: And this was done for the space of two years, so that all which dwelt in Asia, heard the word of our Lord, Jews and Gentiles.
19.11: And God wrought by the hand of Paul miracles not common:
19.12: so that there were also brought from his body napkins or handkerchiefs upon the sick, and the diseases departed from them, and the wicked spirits went out.
19.13: And certain also of the Judaical exorcists that went about, assayed to invocate upon them that had evil spirits, the name of our Lord Jesus, saying: I adjure you by Jesus whom Paul preacheth.
19.14: And there were certain sons of Sceva a Jew, chief Priest, seven, that did this.
19.15: But the wicked spirit answering, said to them: Jesus I know, and Paul I know: but you, what are ye?
19.16: And the man in whom the wicked spirit was, leaping upon them, and mastering both, prevailed against them, so that they fled out of that house naked and wounded.
19.17: And this was made notorious to all the Jews and the Gentiles that dwelt at Ephesus: and fear fell upon all them, and the name of our Lord Jesus was magnified.
19.18: And many of them that believed, came confessing & declaring their deeds.
19.19: And many of them that had followed curious things, brought together their books, and burnt them before all: and counting the prices of them, they found the money to be fifty thousand pence.
19.20: So mightily increased the word of God and was confirmed.
19.21: And when these things were ended, Paul purposed in the Spirit, when he had passed through Macedonia and Achaia, to go to Jerusalem, saying: After I shall have been there, I must see Rome also.
19.22: And sending into Macedonia two of them that ministered unto him, Timothee and Erastus, himself remained for a time in Asia.
19.23: And at that time there was made no little trouble about the way of our Lord.
19.24: For one named Demetrius, a silver-smith, that made silver temples of Diana, procured to the artificers no small gain:
19.25: whom calling together and them that were the same kind of workmen, he said: Sirs, you know that our gain is of this occupation;
19.26: and you see, and hear that this same Paul by persuasion hath averted a great multitude not only of Ephesus, but almost of all Asia, saying: That they are not gods which be made by hands.
19.27: And not only unto us is this part in danger to be reproved, but also the temple of great Diana shall be reputed for nothing, yea & her majesty shall begin to be destroyed, whom all Asia & the world worshippeth.
19.28: Hearing these things they were replenished with anger, and cried out saying: Great is Diana of the Ephesians.
19.29: And the whole city was filled with confusion, and they ran violently with one accord into the theater, catching Gaius & Aristarchus Macedonians, Paul's companions.
19.30: And when Paul would have entered in to the people, the Disciples did not permit him.
19.31: And certain also of the Princes of Asia that were his friends, sent unto him, desiring that he would not adventure himself into the theater:
19.32: and others cried another thing. For the assembly was confuse, & the more part knew not for what cause they were assembled.
19.33: And of the multitude they drew forth Alexander, the Jews thrusting him forward. But Alexander with his hand desiring silence, would have given the people satisfaction.
19.34: Whom as soon as they perceived to be a Jew, there was made one voice of all, almost for the space of two hours crying out: Great is Diana of the Ephesians.
19.35: And when the Scribe had appeased the multitudes, he saith: Ye men of Ephesus, for what man is there that knoweth not the city of the Ephesians to be a worshipper of great Diana, and Jupiter's child?
19.36: Forasmuch therefore as these things cannot be gainsaid, you must be quieted, and do nothing rashly.
19.37: For you have brought these men, being neither sacrilegious, nor blaspheming your goddess.
19.38: But if Demetrius & the artificers that are with him, have matter to say against any man, there are Courts kept in the common place, & there are Proconsuls; let them accuse one another.
19.39: And if you ask any other matter, it may be resolved in a lawful assembly.
19.40: For we are in danger also to be accused for this day's sedition: whereas there is no man guilty by whom we may give an account of this concourse. And when he had said these things, he dismissed the assembly.

20.1: And after that the tumult was ceased, Paul calling the Disciples, and exhorting them, took his leave, and set forward to go into Macedonia.
20.2: And when he had walked through those parts, & had exhorted them with much speech, he came to Greece:
20.3: where when he had spent three months, the Jews laid wait for him as he was about to sail into Syria: and he had counsel to return through Macedonia.
20.4: And there accompanied him Sosipater of Pyrrhus, of Beroea: and of Thessalonians, Aristarchus, and Secundus: and Caius of Derbe, and Timothee: and of Asia, Tychicus and Trophimus.
20.5: These going before, stayed for us at Troas:
20.6: But we sailed after the days of Azymes from Philippi, and came to them unto Troas in five days, where we abode seven days.
20.7: And in the first of the Sabboth when we were assembled to break bread, Paul disputed with them, being to depart on the morrow: and he continued the sermon until mid-night.
20.8: And there were a great number of lamps in the upper chamber where we were assembled.
20.9: And a certain young man named Eutychus, sitting upon the window, whereas he was oppressed with heavy sleep: (Paul disputing long) driven by sleep, fell from the third loft down, and was taken up dead.
20.10: To whom when Paul was gone down, he lay upon him: and embracing him he said: Be not troubled, for his soul is in him.
20.11: And going up and breaking bread and tasting, and having talked sufficiently to them until day light, so he departed.
20.12: And they brought the lad alive, & were not a little comforted.
20.13: But we going up into the ship, sailed to Asson, from thence meaning to receive Paul; for so he had ordained, himself purposing to journey by land.
20.14: And when he had found us in Asson, taking him with us we came to Mitylene.
20.15: And sailing thence, the day following we came over against Chios: and the other day we arrived at Samos: and the day following we came to Miletum.
20.16: For Paul had purposed to sail leaving Ephesus, lest any stay should be made him in Asia. For he hastened, if it were possible for him, to keep the day of Pentecost at Jerusalem.
20.17: And sending from Miletum to Ephesus, he called the Ancients of the Church.
20.18: Who being come to him, and assembled together, he said to them: You know from the first day that I entered into Asia, in what manner I have been with you all the time,
20.19: serving our Lord with all humility and tears, and tentations that did chance to me by the conspiracies of the Jews:
20.20: How I have withdrawn nothing that was profitable, but that I preached it to you, and taught you openly and from house to house,
20.21: testifying to Jews and Gentiles penance toward God, and faith in our Lord Jesus Christ.
20.22: And now behold, being bound by the spirit, I go to Jerusalem; not knowing what things shall befall me in it,
20.23: but that the Holy Ghost throughout all cities doth protest to me, saying: that bands and tribulations abide me at Jerusalem.
20.24: But I fear none of these things, neither do I make my life more precious than myself, so that I may consummate my course & ministry which I received of our Lord Jesus, to testify the Gospel of the grace of God.
20.25: And now behold I do know, that you shall no more see my face all you, through whom I have passed preaching the Kingdom of God.
20.26: Wherefore I take you to witness this present day that I am clear from the blood of all.
20.27: For I have not spared to declare unto you all the counsel of God.
20.28: Take heed to yourselves and to the whole flock wherein the Holy Ghost hath placed you bishops, to rule the Church of God which he hath purchased with his own blood.
20.29: I know that after my departure there will ravening wolves enter in among you, not sparing the flock.
20.30: And out of your own selves shall arise men speaking perverse things, to draw away Disciples after themselves.
20.31: For the which cause be vigilant, keeping in memory that for three years night & day I ceased not with tears to admonish every one of you.
20.32: And now I commend you to God and to the word of his grace, who is able to edify, and to give inheritance in all the sanctified.
20.33: No man's silver and gold or garment have I coveted.
20.34: Yourselves know that for such things as were needful for me and them that are with me, these hands have ministered.
20.35: I have shewed you all things, that so labouring, you must receive the weak, and remember the word of our Lord Jesus, because he said: It is a more blessed thing to give rather than to take.
20.36: And when he had said these things, falling on his knees he prayed with all them.
20.37: And there was great weeping made of all; and falling upon the neck of Paul, they kissed him,
20.38: being sorry most of all for the word which he had said, that they should see his face no more. And they brought him going unto the ship.

21.1: And when it came to pass that we sailed, being carried from them, with a straight course we came to Coos, and the day following to Rhodes, and from thence to Patara.
21.2: And when we had found a ship that passed over to Phoenice, going up into it we sailed.
21.3: And when we were in the sight of Cypres, leaving it on the left hand, we sailed into Syria, and came to Tyre: for there the ship was to discharge her load.
21.4: And finding Disciples, we tarried there seven days: who said to Paul by the Spirit, that he should not go up to Jerusalem.
21.5: And the days being expired, departing we went forward, all bringing us on the way, with their wives and children, till we were out of the city: and falling upon our knees on the shore, we prayed.
21.6: And when we had bid one another farewell, we went up into the ship; and they returned unto their own.
21.7: But we having ended the navigation, from Tyre came down to Ptolomais: and saluting the brethren, we tarried one day with them.
21.8: And the next day departing, we came to Cæsarea. And entering into the house of Philip the Evangelist, who was one of the seven, we tarried with him.
21.9: And he had four daughters virgins, that did prophesy.
21.10: And as we abode there for certain days, there came a certain Prophet from Jewry, named Agabus.
21.11: He, when he was come to us, took Paul's girdle: and binding his own feet & hands, he said: Thus saith the Holy Ghost: The man whose girdle this is, so shall the Jews bind in Jerusalem, and shall deliver him into the hands of the Gentiles.
21.12: Which when we had heard, we and they that were of the same place, desired him that he would not go up to Jerusalem.
21.13: Then Paul answered, and said: What do you, weeping and afflicting my heart? for I am ready not only to be bound, but to die also in Jerusalem for the name of our Lord Jesus.
21.14: And when we could not persuade him, we ceased, saying: The will of our Lord be done.
21.15: And after these days, being prepared, we went up to Jerusalem.
21.16: And there came also of the Disciples from Cæsarea with us, bringing with them one Jason a Cyprian (with whom we should lodge) an old Disciple.
21.17: And when we were come to Jerusalem, the brethren received us gladly.
21.18: And the day following Paul went in with us to James, and all the Ancients were assembled.
21.19: Whom when he had saluted, he told particularly what God had done among the Gentiles by his ministry.
21.20: But they hearing it, magnified God, and said to him: Thou seest (brother) how many thousands there are among the Jews that have believed: and all are zelatours of the Law.
21.21: But they have heard of thee that thou doest teach those Jews that are among the Gentiles, to depart from Moyses: saying that they ought not to circumcise their children, nor walk according to the custom.
21.22: What is it then? needs must the multitude assemble: for they will hear that thou art come.
21.23: Do this therefore which we tell thee. There are with us four men, that have a vow on them.
21.24: Taking these unto thee, sanctify thyself with them; and bestow on them, that they may shave their heads: and all shall know that the things which they heard of thee, are false: but that thyself also walkest keeping the Law.
21.25: But concerning them that believe of the Gentiles, we have written, decreeing that they should refrain themselves from the immolated to idols, and blood, and suffocated, and fornication.
21.26: Then Paul taking the men unto him, the next day being purified with them entered into the temple, shewing the accomplishment of the days of the purification, until an oblation was offered for every one of them.
21.27: But whiles the seven days were a finishing, those Jews that were of Asia, when they had seen him in the temple, stirred up all the people, and laid hands upon him,
21.28: crying: Ye men of Israel, help: this is the man that against the people and the Law and this place teaching all men every-where, hath also moreover brought in Gentiles into the temple, and hath violated this holy place.
21.29: (For they had seen Trophimus the Ephesian in the city with him, whom they supposed that Paul had brought into the temple.)
21.30: And the whole city was in an uproar: and there was made a concourse of the people. And apprehending Paul, they drew him forth of the temple: and immediately the doors were shut.
21.31: And as they sought to kill him, it was told the Tribune of the band, That all Jerusalem is in a confusion.
21.32: Who forthwith taking unto him soldiers and Centurions, ran down to them. Who, when they had seen the Tribune and the soldiers, ceased to strike Paul.
21.33: Then the Tribune coming near apprehended him, and commanded him to be bound with two chains: and he demanded who he was, and what he had done.
21.34: And some cried one thing, some another, in the multitude. And whereas he could not know the certainty for the tumult, he commanded him to be led into the castle.
21.35: And when he was come to the stairs, it chanced that he was carried of the soldiers because of the violence of the people.
21.36: For the multitude of the people followed, crying: Away with him.
21.37: And when Paul began to be brought into the castle, he saith to the Tribune: is it lawful for me to speak something to thee? Who said: Canst thou speak Greek?
21.38: Art not thou the Ægyptian that before these days did raise a tumult, and didst lead forth into the desert four thousand men that were murderers?
21.39: And Paul said to him: I am a man truly a Jew of Tarsus, a citizen not of an obscure city of Cilicia. And I desire thee, permit me to speak to the people.
21.40: And when he had permitted him, Paul standing on the stairs, beckoned with his hand to the people. And great silence being made, he spake unto them in the Hebrew tongue, saying.

22.1: Men brethren and fathers, hear what account I do render now unto you.
22.2: (And when they had heard that he spake to them in the Hebrew tongue, they did the more keep silence.
22.3: And he saith:) I am a man a Jew, born at Tarsus in Cilicia, but brought up in this city, at the feet of Gamaliel instructed according to the verity of the law of the fathers, an emulator of the Law as also all you are this day:
22.4: who persecuted this way unto death, binding & delivering into custodies men & women,
22.5: as the high Priest doth give me testimony, and all the Ancients. Of whom receiving letters also to the brethren, I went to Damascus, that I might bring them thence bound to Jerusalem, to be punished.
22.6: And it came to pass as I was going, and drawing nigh to Damascus at mid-day, suddenly from Heaven there shone round about me much light:
22.7: and falling on the ground, I heard a voice saying to me: Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?
22.8: And I answered: Who are thou Lord? And he said to me: I am Jesus of Nazareth, whom thou persecutest.
22.9: And they that were with me, saw the light indeed, but the voice they heard not of him that spake with me.
22.10: And I said: What shall I do Lord? And our Lord said to me: Arise and go to Damascus; and there it shall be told thee of all things that thou must do.
22.11: And whereas I did not see for the brightness of that light, being led of my companions by the hand, I came to Damascus.
22.12: And one Ananias, a man according to the Law, having testimony of all the Jews' inhabitants,
22.13: coming to me, and standing by me, said to me: Brother Saul, look up. And I the self-same hour looked upon him.
22.14: But he said: The God of our fathers hath preordained thee, that thou shouldest know his will, and see the just one, and hear a voice from his mouth:
22.15: because thou shalt be his witness to all men, of those things which thou hast seen and heard.
22.16: And now what tarriest thou? Rise up, and be baptized, and wash away thy sins invocating his name.
22.17: And it befell me returning into Jerusalem, and praying in the temple, that I was in a trance,
22.18: and saw him saying unto me: Make haste, and depart quickly out of Jerusalem: because they will not receive thy testimony of me.
22.19: And I said, Lord, they know that I did cast into prison and beat in every Synagogue them that believed in thee.
22.20: And when the blood of Steven thy witness was shed, I stood by and consented, and kept the garments of them that killed him.
22.21: And he said to me: Go, for into the Gentiles a far will I send thee.
22.22: And they heard him until this word, and they lifted up their voice, saying: Away with such an one from the earth: for it is not meet he should live.
22.23: And when they cried out, and threw off their garments, and cast dust into the air,
22.24: the Tribune commanded him to be carried into the castle, and to be beaten with whips, & that he should be tormented: to know for what cause they did so cry at him.
22.25: And when they had bound him very straight with thongs, Paul saith to the Centurion standing by him: Is it lawful for you to whip a man that is a Roman and uncondemned?
22.26: Which the Centurion hearing, went to the Tribune, and told him, saying: What wilt thou do? for this man is a citizen of Rome.
22.27: And the Tribune coming, said to him: Tell me, art thou a Roman? But he said: Yea.
22.28: And the Tribune answered: I obtained this city with a great sum. And Paul said: But I was also born to it.
22.29: Immediately therefore they departed from him that were to torment him. The Tribune also feared after he understood that he was a citizen of Rome, and because he had bound him.
22.30: But the next day meaning to know more diligently for what cause he was accused of the Jews, he loosed him, and commanded the Priests to come together and all the Council: & bringing forth Paul, he set him among them.

23.1: And Paul looking upon the Council, said: Men brethren, I with all good conscience have conversed before God, until this present day.
23.2: And the high Priest Ananias commanded them that stood by him, to smite him on the mouth.
23.3: Then Paul said to him: God shall strike thee, thou whited wall. And thou sitting judgest me according to the law, and contrary to law doest thou command me to be smitten?
23.4: And they that stood by, said: Doest thou revile the high Priest of God?
23.5: And Paul said: I knew not, brethren, that he is the high Priest. For it is written: The prince of thy people thou shalt not misspeak.
23.6: And Paul knowing that the one part was of Sadducees, and the other of Pharisees, he cried out in the Council: Men brethren, I am a Pharisee, the son of Pharisees: of the hope and resurrection of the dead am I judged.
23.7: And when he had said these things, there rose dissension between the Pharisees and Sadducees; and the multitude was divided.
23.8: For the Sadducees say there is no resurrection, nor Angel, nor spirit: but the Pharisees confess both.
23.9: And there was made a great cry. And certain of the Pharisees rising up, strove saying: We find no evil in this man. What if a spirit hath spoken to him, or an Angel?
23.10: And when there was risen great dissension, the Tribune fearing lest Paul should be torn in pieces by them, commanded the soldiers to go down, and to take him out of the midst of them, and to bring him into the castle.
23.11: And the night following our Lord standing by him, said: Be constant; for as thou hast testified of me in Jerusalem, so must thou testify at Rome also.
23.12: And when day was come, certain of the Jews gathered themselves together, & vowed themselves, saying: that they would neither eat nor drink till they killed Paul.
23.13: And they were more than forty men that had made this conspiracy:
23.14: who came to the chief priests and the ancients, and said: By execration we have vowed ourselves, that we will eat nothing, till we kill Paul.
23.15: Now therefore give you knowledge to the Tribune with the Council, that he bring him forth to you, as if you meant to know some more certainty touching him. But we, before he come near, are ready for to kill him.
23.16: Which when Paul's sister's son had heard, of their lying in wait, he came and entered into the castle and told Paul.
23.17: And Paul calling to him one of the Centurions, said: Bring this young man to the Tribune, for he hath something to tell him.
23.18: And he taking him, brought him to the Tribune, and said: The prisoner Paul desired me to bring this young man unto thee, having something to say to thee.
23.19: And the Tribune taking him by the hand, went aside with him apart, and asked him: What is it that thou hast to tell me?
23.20: And he said: The Jews have agreed to desire thee, that tomorrow thou wilt bring forth Paul into the Council, as though they meant to inquire some more certainty touching him.
23.21: But do not thou credit them; for there lie in wait for him more than forty men of them, which have vowed neither to eat nor to drink, till they kill him: and they are now ready, expecting thy promise.
23.22: The Tribune therefore dismissed the young man, commanding that he should speak to no man that he had notified these things unto him.
23.23: And calling two Centurions, he said to them: Make ready two hundred soldiers, to go as far as Cæsarea, and seventy horsemen, and lances two hundred, from the third hour of the night:
23.24: and prepare beasts: that setting Paul on, they might bring him safe to Felix the President.
23.25: (For he feared lest perhaps the Jews might take him away, and kill him, and himself afterward should sustain reproach, as though he would have taken money)
23.26: writing a letter containing this much: Claudius Lysias to the most excellent President Felix, greeting.
23.27: This man being apprehended of the Jews, and ready to be killed of them, I coming in with the band delivered him, understanding that he is a Roman:
23.28: and meaning to know the cause that they objected unto him, I brought him down into their Council.
23.29: Whom I found to be accused concerning questions of their law: but having no crime worthy of death or of bands.
23.30: And when it was told me of ambushments that they had prepared against him, I sent him to thee, signifying also to the accusers, to speak before thee. Fare-well.
23.31: And the soldiers according as it was commanded them, taking Paul, brought him by night to Antipatris.
23.32: And the next day sending away the horse-men to go with him, they returned to the castle.
23.33: Who when they were come to Cæsarea, and had delivered the letter to the President, they did set Paul also before him.
23.34: And when he had read, and had asked of what province he was: and understanding that of Cilicia:
23.35: I will hear thee, said he, when thy accusers are come. And he commanded him to be kept in Herod's palace.

24.1: And after five days the high priest Ananias descended, with certain ancients and one Tertullus an orator, who went to the President against Paul.
24.2: And Paul being cited, Tertullus began to accuse, saying: Whereas we live in much peace by thee, & many things are corrected by thy providence,
24.3: we do always & in all places receive it, most excellent Felix, with all thanks-giving.
24.4: But lest I hinder thee any longer, I desire thee of thy clemency briefly to hear us.
24.5: We have found this man pestiferous, & raising seditions to all the Jews in the whole world, and author of the sedition of the sect of the Nazarenes.
24.6: Who also hath attempted to violate the temple, whom also being apprehended we would have judged according to our law.
24.7: But Lysias the Tribune coming in, with great force took him away out of our hands,
24.8: commanding his accusers to come to thee, of whom thou mayest thyself judging, understand of all these things, whereof we accuse him.
24.9: And the Jews also added, saying that these things were so.
24.10: But Paul answered: (the President making a sign unto him for to speak) Knowing that of many years thou art judge over this nation, I will with good courage answer for myself.
24.11: For thou mayest understand that it is not above twelve days to me, since I went up to adore in Jerusalem.
24.12: And neither in the temple did they find me disputing with any man, or causing concourse of the multitude, neither in the synagogues, nor in the city:
24.13: neither can they prove unto thee the things whereof they now accuse me.
24.14: But this I confess to thee, that according to the sect, which they call heresy, I do so serve the Father my God, believing all things that are written in the Law & the Prophets:
24.15: having hope in God, the which these also themselves expect, that there shall be a resurrection of just and unjust.
24.16: In this myself also do study to have a conscience without offence toward God and toward men always.
24.17: And after many years I came to bestow alms upon my nation, & oblations, and vows.
24.18: In the which they found me purified in the temple: not with multitude nor with tumult.
24.19: But certain Jews of Asia, who ought to be present before thee and to accuse, if they had any thing against me:
24.20: or let these men themselves say, if they have found in me any iniquity, forasmuch as I stand in the Council,
24.21: but of this one voice only that I cried standing among them, That of the resurrection of the dead am I judged this day of you.
24.22: And Felix deferred them, knowing most certainly of this way, saying: When Lysias the Tribune is come down, I will hear you.
24.23: And he commanded the Centurion to keep him, and that he should have rest, neither to prohibit any of his to minister unto him.
24.24: And after some days, Felix coming with Drusilla his wife, which was a Jew, called Paul, and heard of him the faith that is in Christ Jesus.
24.25: And he disputing of justice and chastity, and of the judgement to come, Felix being terrified, answered: For this time, go thy way: but in time convenient I will send for thee.
24.26: hoping also withal, that money would be given him of Paul; for the which cause also oftentimes sending for him, he spake with him.
24.27: But when two years were ended, Felix had a Successor Portius Festus. And Felix being willing to shew the Jews a pleasure, left Paul in prison.

25.1: Festus therefore when he was come into the province, after three days went up to Jerusalem from Cæsarea.
25.2: And the chief Priests, and principal men of the Jews went unto him against Paul: and they desired him,
25.3: requesting favour against him, that he would command him to be brought to Jerusalem, laying wait for to kill him in the way.
25.4: But Festus answered, that Paul is kept in Cæsarea: and that he would very shortly go thither.
25.5: They therefore, saith he, that are of ability among you, going down with me, if there be any crime in the man, let them accuse him.
25.6: And having tarried among them not above eight or ten days, he went down to Cæsarea, and the next day he sat in the judgement seat: and he commanded Paul to be brought.
25.7: Who being brought, there stood about him the Jews that were come down from Jerusalem, objecting many and grievous causes which they could not prove;
25.8: Paul making answer, That neither against the law of the Jews, nor against the temple, nor against Cæsar have I any thing offended.
25.9: But Festus willing to shew the Jews a pleasure, answering Paul, said: Wilt thou go up to Jerusalem, and there be judged of these things before me?
25.10: And Paul said: At Cæsar's judgement seat do I stand, where I ought to be judged: the Jews I have not hurt, as thou very well knowest.
25.11: For if I have hurt them, or done any thing worthy of death, I refuse not to die. But if none of those things be, whereof these accuse me, no man can give me to them. I appeal to Cæsar.
25.12: Then Festus having conferred with the Council, answered: Hast thou appealed to Cæsar? to Cæsar shalt thou go.
25.13: And when certain days were passed, king Agrippa & Bernice came down to Cæsarea to salute Festus.
25.14: And as they tarried there a good many days, Festus signified to the king, of Paul, saying: A certain person was left prisoner by Felix,
25.15: concerning whom, when I was at Jerusalem, the chief Priests and the Ancients of the Jews came unto me, desiring condemnation against him.
25.16: To whom I answered: That it is not the Romans' custom to yield up any man before that he which is accused have his accusers present, and take place to make his answer for to clear himself of the crimes.
25.17: When they therefore were assembled hither, without any delay, the day following, sitting in the judgement seat, I commanded the man to be brought.
25.18: Of whom, when the accusers stood up, they brought no cause which I thought ill of:
25.19: but certain questions of their own superstition they had against him, and of one Jesus deceased, whom Paul affirmed to live.
25.20: Doubting therefore of this kind of question, I said, whether he would go to Jerusalem, & there be judged of these things.
25.21: But Paul appealing to be kept unto the knowledge of Augustus, I commanded him to be kept, till I send him to Cæsar.
25.22: And Agrippa said to Festus: Myself also would hear the man. Tomorrow, said he, thou shalt hear him.
25.23: And the next day when Agrippa and Bernice were come with great pomp, and had entered into the hall of audience with the Tribunes and principal men of the city, at Festus' commandment Paul was brought.
25.24: And Festus saith: King Agrippa, and all ye men that are present together with us, you see this man, concerning whom all the multitude of the Jews called upon me at Jerusalem, requesting and crying out that he ought not to live any longer.
25.25: Yet have I found nothing that he hath committed worthy of death. But forasmuch as he himself appealed to Augustus, I have determined to send him.
25.26: Of whom what to write for certainty to my lord, I have not. For the which cause I have brought him forth to you, and especially to thee, king Agrippa, that examination being made, I may have what to write.
25.27: For it seemeth to me without reason, to send a prisoner, & not to signify his causes.

26.1: But Agrippa said to Paul: Thou art permitted to speak for thyself. Then Paul stretching forth his hand, began to make his answer:
26.2: Touching all things whereof I am accused of the Jews, king Agrippa, I account myself happy for that I am to defend myself this day before thee,
26.3: especially whereas thou knowest all things that are among the Jews, customs and questions: for the which cause I beseech thee, hear me patiently.
26.4: And my life truly from my youth, which was from the beginning in my nation in Jerusalem, all the Jews do know:
26.5: knowing me before from the beginning (if they will give testimony) that according to the most sure sect of our religion I lived a Pharisee.
26.6: And now for the hope of the promise that was made of God to our fathers, do I stand subject to judgement:
26.7: the which, our twelve tribes serving night and day, hope to come unto. Of the which hope, o king, I am accused of the Jews.
26.8: What incredible thing is it judged with you, if God raise the dead?
26.9: And myself truly had thought that I ought to do against the name of Jesus of Nazareth many contrary things.
26.10: Which also I did at Jerusalem, and many of the saints did I shut up in prisons, having received authority of the chief priests: and when they were put to death, I brought the sentence.
26.11: And throughout all the synagogues oftentimes punishing them, I compelled them to blaspheme: and yet more mad against them, I persecuted them even unto foreign cities.
26.12: Among which things whiles I went to Damascus with authority and permission of the chief priests,
26.13: at mid-day, in the way, I saw (o king) from Heaven a light to have shined round about me and them that were in company with me, above the brightness of the sun.
26.14: And when all we were fallen down on the ground, I heard a voice speaking to me in the Hebrew tongue: Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me? It is hard for thee to kick against the prick.
26.15: And I said: Who are thou Lord? And our Lord answered: I am Jesus whom thou doest persecute.
26.16: But rise up and stand upon thy feet: for to this end have I appeared to thee, that I may ordain thee a minister and witness of those things which thou hast seen, and of those things wherein I will appear to thee,
26.17: delivering thee out of the peoples and nations unto the which now I send thee,
26.18: to open their eyes, that they may be converted from darkness to light, and from the power of Satan to God, that they may receive remission of sins, and lot among the saints by the faith that is in me.
26.19: Whereupon, king Agrippa, I was not incredulous to the heavenly vision:
26.20: but to them first that are at Damascus, and at Jerusalem, and unto all the country of Jewry, and to the Gentiles did I preach that they should do penance, & turn to God, doing works worthy of penance.
26.21: For this cause the Jews, when I was in the temple, apprehending me, attempted meaning to kill me.
26.22: But aided by the help of God, I stand until this day, testifying to small & to great, saying nothing beside those things which the Prophets did speak should come to pass, & Moyses;
26.23: if Christ were passible, if the first of the resurrection from the dead, he were to shew light to the people and to the Gentiles.
26.24: As he spake these things and made his answer, Festus with a loud voice said: Thou art mad, Paul; much learning turneth thee to madness.
26.25: And Paul said: I am not mad, most excellent Festus; but I speak words of verity & sobriety.
26.26: For the king knoweth of these things, to whom also I speak constantly. For I think none of these things to be unknown to him. For neither was any of these things done in a corner.
26.27: Believest thou the Prophets, king Agrippa? I know that thou believest.
26.28: And Agrippa said to Paul: A little thou persuadest me to become a Christian.
26.29: And Paul said: I wish of God, both in little, and in much, not only thee, but also all that hear this day, to become such as I am also, except these bands.
26.30: And the king rose up, and the President, and Bernice, and they that sat by them.
26.31: And going aside, they spake among themselves, saying, That this man hath done nothing worthy of death or bands.
26.32: And Agrippa said to Festus: This man might be released, if he had not appealed to Cæsar.

27.1: And after it was decreed that he should sail into Italy, and that Paul with other prisoners should be delivered to a Centurion named Julius, of the band Augusta,
27.2: we going up into a ship of Adrumetum, beginning to sail about the places of Asia, loosed from the land, Aristarchus the Macedonian of Thessalonica continuing with us.
27.3: And the third day following we came to Sidon. And Julius entreating Paul courteously, permitted him to go to his friends, and to take care of himself.
27.4: And when we had loosed thence we sailed under Cypres: because the winds were contrary.
27.5: And sailing the sea of Cilicia and Pamphilia, we came to Lystra, which is in Lycia:
27.6: and there the Centurion finding a ship of Alexandria sailing into Italy, removed us into it.
27.7: And whereas many days we sailed slowly, and were scarce come over against Gnidus, the wind hindering us, we sailed near Crete by Salmone:
27.8: and with much ado sailing by it, we came into a certain place that is called Good-havens, nigh to the which was a city Thalassa.
27.9: And when much time was spent, and whereas now it was not safe sailing, because the fast now was past, Paul comforted them,
27.10: saying to them: Ye men, I see that the sailing beginneth to be with hurt and much damage, not only of the lading and the ship, but also of our lives.
27.11: But the Centurion believed the governor and master of the ship, more than those things which were said of Paul.
27.12: And whereas it was not a commodious haven to winter in, very many taking counsel appointed to sail thence, if by any means they might coming to Phoenice, winter there, a haven of Crete looking toward the Afrike and the Chore.
27.13: And the south-wind blowing, they thinking that they had obtained their purpose, when they had parted from Asson, sailed along by Crete.
27.14: But not long after, a tempestuous wind that is called Euro-aquilo, drove against it.
27.15: And when the ship was caught and could not make way against the wind, giving up the ship to the winds, we were driven,
27.16: And running upon a certain Island, that is called Cauda, we could scarce get the cock-boat.
27.17: Which being taken up, they used helps, girding the ship, and fearing lest they should fall into the Syrt, letting down the vessel, so were they carried.
27.18: And when we were mightily tossed with the tempest, the next day they cast forth.
27.19: And the third day with their own hands they threw forth the tacklings of the ship.
27.20: And neither sun, nor stars appearing for many days, and no small storm being toward, all hope was now taken away of our saving.
27.21: And when there had been long fasting, then Paul standing in the midst of them, said: You should indeed, O ye men, have heard me, and not have parted from Crete, & have gained this hurt and loss.
27.22: And now I exhort you to be of good cheer. For there shall be no loss of any soul among you, but of the ship.
27.23: For an Angel of the God whose I am, and whom I serve, stood by me this night,
27.24: saying: Fear not Paul, thou must appear before Cæsar: and behold God hath given thee all that sail with thee.
27.25: For the which cause be of good cheer ye men: for I believe God, that it shall so be, as it hath been said to me.
27.26: And we must come unto a certain island.
27.27: But after the fourteenth night was come on us, as we were sailing in Adria about mid-night, the ship-men deemed that there appeared some country to them.
27.28: Who also sounding, found twenty fathoms: and being parted a little from thence, they found fifteen fathoms.
27.29: And fearing lest we should fall into rough places, casting out of the stern four anchors, they wished that day were come.
27.30: But as the ship-men sought to fly out of the ship, having let down the cock-boat into the sea, pretending as if they were about to cast out anchors out of the fore-part of the ship,
27.31: Paul said to the Centurion and to the soldiers: unless these tarry in the ship you cannot be saved.
27.32: Then the soldiers cut off the ropes of the cock-boat; and suffered it to fall away.
27.33: And when it began to be light, Paul desired all to take meat, saying: This day is the fourteenth day that you expect and remain fasting, taking nothing.
27.34: For the which cause I desire you to take meat for your health sake: for there shall not an hair of the head perish of any of you.
27.35: And when he had said these things, taking bread he gave thanks to God in the sight of them all: and when he had broken it, he began to eat.
27.36: And being all made of better cheer, they also took meat.
27.37: And we were in all in the ship, souls two hundred seventy six.
27.38: And being filled with meat, they lighted the ship, casting the wheat into the sea.
27.39: And when day was come, they knew not the land: but they spied a certain creek that had a shore, into the which they minded, if they could, to cast aland the ship.
27.40: And when they had taken up the anchors, they committed themselves to the sea, loosing withal the rudder bands: and hoisting up the main sail according as the wind blew, they went on toward the shore.
27.41: And when we were fallen into a place between two seas, they graveled the ship: and the fore-part truly sticking fast remained unmoveable: but the hinder-part was broken by the violence of the sea.
27.42: And the counsel of the soldiers was, that they should kill the prisoners: lest any swimming out, might run away.
27.43: But the Centurion willing to save Paul, forbade it to be done: & he commanded them that could swim, to cast out themselves first, and escape, and go forth to land:
27.44: and the rest, some they carried on boards, & some upon those things that were of the ship. And so it came to pass, that all the souls escaped to land.

28.1: And when we had escaped, then we knew that the island was called Mitilene. But the Barbarous shewed us no small courtesy.
28.2: For, kindling a fire they refreshed us all, because of the imminent rain and the cold.
28.3: And when Paul had gathered together some number of sticks, and had laid them on the fire, a viper issuing out of the heat, invaded his hand.
28.4: But as the Barbarous saw the beast hanging on his hand, they said one to another: Undoubtedly this man is a murderer, who being escaped out of the sea, vengeance doth not suffer him to live.
28.5: and he indeed shaking off the beast into the fire, suffered no harm.
28.6: But they supposed that he should be turned into a swelling, and that he would suddenly fall and die. But expecting long and seeing that there was no harm done on him, being changed they said, that he was a God.
28.7: And in those places were lands of the prince of the isle, named Publius, who receiving us, for three days entreated us courteously.
28.8: And it chanced that the father of Publius lay vexed with fevers and the bloody flux. Unto whom Paul entered: and when he had prayed, and imposed hands on him, he healed him.
28.9: Which being done, all in the isle also that had infirmities, came, and were cured:
28.10: who also honoured us with many honours, and when we were sailing away, laded us with necessaries.
28.11: And after three months, we sailed in a ship of Alexandria, that had wintered in the island, whose sign was the Castors.
28.12: And when we were come to Syracusa, we tarried there three days.
28.13: Thence compassing by the shore, we came to Rhegium: and after one day the South-wind blowing, we came the second day to Puteoli,
28.14: where finding brethren, we were desired to tarry with them seven days: and so we came to Rome.
28.15: And from thence, when the Brethren had heard, they came to meet us unto Apij-forum, and the Three-taverns, whom when Paul had seen, giving thanks to God, he took courage.
28.16: And when we were come to Rome, Paul was permitted to remain to himself with a soldier that kept him.
28.17: And after the third day he called together the chief of the Jews. And when they were assembled, he said to them: Men brethren, I doing nothing against the people, or the custom of the fathers, was delivered prisoner from Jerusalem into the hands of the Romans,
28.18: who when they had examined me, would have released me, for that there was no cause of death in me.
28.19: But the Jews contradicting it, I was compelled to appeal unto Cæsar, not as having any thing to accuse my nation.
28.20: For this cause therefore I desired to see you and to speak to you. For, because of the hope of Israel, am I compassed with this chain.
28.21: But they said to him: We neither received letters concerning thee from Jewry, neither did any of the brethren that came hither, report or speak any evil of thee.
28.22: But we desire of thee to hear what thou thinkest: for concerning this sect, it is known to us that it is gainsaid every-where.
28.23: And when they had appointed him a day, they came to him unto his lodging very many: to whom he expounded, testifying the Kingdom of God, and using persuasion to them of Jesus, out of the Law of Moyses and the Prophets, from morning until evening.
28.24: And certain believed those things that were said: but certain believed not.
28.25: And whereas they did not agree among themselves, they departed, Paul saying one word: That well did the Holy Ghost speak by Esaie the Prophet to our fathers,
28.26: saying: Go to this people, and say to them: With the ear you shall hear, and shall not understand: and seeing you shall see and shall not perceive.
28.27: For the heart of this people is waxen gross, and with their ears have they heavily heard, and their eyes they have shut: lest perhaps they may see with their eyes, and hear with their ears, and understand with their heart, and be converted, and I heal them.
28.28: Be it known therefore to you, that this Salvation of God is sent to the Gentiles, and they will hear.
28.29: And when he had said these things, the Jews went out from him, having much questioning among themselves.
28.30: And he tarried full two years in his hired lodging: and he received all that came in to him,
28.31: preaching the Kingdom of God, and teaching the things that concern our Lord Jesus Christ with all confidence, without prohibition.
